rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT ET AL VS LAIDLAW TRANSIT INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant First Student, Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Evidence Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 22, 2015, Line 3, Defendant First Student, Inc.'s Motion For Evidence Sanctions. Judge Kahn will hear this motion at 9:15 am in Dept. 302. Judge Kahn has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant First Student Inc.'s motion for evidence sanctions is denied without prejudice to being re-filed before the trial judge. While the motion may be handled as a discovery matter prior to assignment of the case to a trial department, the better practice is to have a motion such as this, which seeks to preclude evidence introduced at trial that was requested during discovery, decided by the trial judge on a motion in limine because the trial judge will be familiar with all the relevant facts and will enforce any order that is made. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: RAIN O DAUGHERTY VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Order Staying Administrative Proceedings And To Reject Jurisdiction (Part 1 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 22, 2015, Line 7, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, SAN FRANCISCO POLICE DEPARTMENT, SAN FRANCISCO POLICE COMMISSION'S Motion To Vacate Order Staying Administrative Proceedings And To Reject Jurisdiction (Part 1 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). Hearing required. The parties are instructed to meet and confer prior to the hearing regarding a mutually agreeable hearing date for the writ, as well as a proposed briefing schedule. The Court is inclined to rule on the motion to vacate the stay order as follows: Denied. The Peace Officers' Procedural Bill of Rights ("POBR"), codified at Gov. Code secs. 3300 et seq. provides due process protections for peace officers. Gov. Code sec. 3309.5(c) provides that the "superior court shall have initial jurisdiction over any proceeding brought by any public safety officer against any public safety department for alleged violations of this chapter." Section 3309.5(d)(1) also provides that if the superior court determines that a public safety department has violated any of the provisions of this chapter, "the court shall render appropriate injunctive relief or other extraordinary relief to remedy the violation¿including, but not limited to, the granting of a temporary restraining order, preliminary injunction, or permanent injunction prohibiting the public safety department from taking any punitive action against the public safety officer." Petitioners' ex parte application sets forth sufficient evidence establishing that the City is in violation of Gov. Code sec. 3304(d)(1), which provides that "no punitive action¿shall be undertaken for any act, omission, or other allegation of misconduct if the investigation of the allegation is not completed within one year of the public agency's discovery by a person authorized to initiate an investigation of the allegation of an act, omission, or other misconduct." The evidence shows that the City has undertaken punitive action against Petitioners, as defined in Gov. Code sec. 3303. (Declaration of Alison Berry Wilkinson, Ex. D; Declaration of Rain Daugherty, par. 7.) Additionally, Petitioners submit evidence showing that the City is acting beyond the one-year statute of limitations. (Wilkinson Decl., pars. 14, 29, Ex. F.) In its opposition to the ex parte application, the City argues that the Police Chief did not learn about the text messages until January 2015. However, the evidence submitted is insufficient to establish this fact, as Ashley Worsham, an attorney in the Internal Affairs Division of the SFPD, states that she is "informed and believed that Police Chief Sur did not know about the text messages until January 2015." (Declaration of Ashley Worsham, par. 13.) This statement lacks foundation and is based on hearsay. The City also argues that the criminal investigation exception set forth at Gov. Code §3304(d)(2)(A) applies. Again, however, insufficient evidence is set forth to substantiate this argument. The Declaration of Ashley Worsham does not establish that the text messages at issue in this case were the subject of the criminal investigation against Officer Furminger. Thus, the City has not met its burden of showing that the statute of limitations does not bar the discipline. (Continued in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: RAIN O DAUGHERTY VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order And/Or To Seal Records Protected By Penal Code Section 832.7
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 22, 2015, Line 7, PLAINTIFF RAIN DAUGHERTY'S Motion For Protective Order And/Or To Seal Records Protected By Penal Code Section 832.7. Granted in part. All references to material protected by Penal Code sections 832.7 and 832.8 must be filed under seal pursuant to the procedures set forth in Rule 2.550 and 2.551. The Petitioners who have not been previously identified in Court filings may continue to be identified in both court filings and oral proceedings only by their Internal Affairs Division Case Numbers. Copley Press, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (2006) 39 Cal.4th 1272, 1296 establishes that the Legislature did not intend for an officer to have to choose between protecting his rights via an action under the Peace Officers Bill of Rights and his right of confidentiality as set forth in the Penal Code. The request for an order closing the hearings is denied without prejudice. Petitioners have not adequately established that the requirements for closure, as set forth in NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1217-18, are met. This ruling is without prejudice to Petitioners filing a noticed motion seeking this relief. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: RAIN O DAUGHERTY VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Order Staying Administrative Proceedings And To Reject Jurisdiction (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 22, 2015, Line 7, Motion To Vacate Order Staying Administrative Proceedings And To Reject Jurisdiction (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). (Continued from Part 1 of 2) Accordingly, Petitioners have set forth sufficient evidence of a likelihood of prevailing on the POBR claim. The Court is therefore able to render appropriate relief, pursuant to Gov. Code sec. 3309.5(d)(1). (See Alameida v. State Personnel Bd. (2004) 120 Cal.App.4th 46, 54, "Once punitive action is taken, the employee can assert violation of the Act as a defense to discipline in the administrative proceedings, or can seek an adjudication in court.") The stay order issued ex parte on May 19, 2015, shall remain in effect, pending a final adjudication of the writ. The Court finds it is in the interest of justice to stay the administrative proceeding, rather than have the statute of limitations issue adjudicated in two forums, with a potential for conflicting results. rulings.Case Title: JANET KASSOUF VS. RICHARD WEAVER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 22, 2015, Line 10, CROSS DEFENDANT JANET KASSOUF'S DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT. The motion is sustained without leave to amend against Plaintiff Kassouf. Cross Complainants cite no authority under California law allowing them to allege equitable indemnity against Plaintiff. The doctrine of equitable indemnity applies among defendants who are jointly and severally liable to the plaintiff. [See GEM Developers v. Hallcraft Homes of San Diego, Inc. (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 419, 430; also BFGC Architects Planners, Inc. v. Forcum/Mackey Constr., Inc., (2004) 119 Cal. App. 4th 848, 852; also Greystone Homes, Inc. v. Midtec, Inc. (2008) 168 Cal. App. 4th 1194, 1218.] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: STEPHEN HEATH VS. FREDRICKSON, MAZEIKA & GRANT, LLP ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Notice Of Petition And Petition To Compel Binding Arbitration, Stay State Court Proceedings, And Transfer State Court Jurisdiction To The San Diego County Court
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 22, 2015, Line 12, DEFENDANT FREDRICKSON, MAZEIKA & GRANT, LLP, TIMOTHY GRANT'S Petition To Compel Binding Arbitration, Stay State Court Proceedings, And Transfer State Court Jurisdiction To The San Diego County Court. The motion to compel arbitration is granted, the court stays this action, and the court denies the motion to transfer venue to San Diego. Defendant sufficiently establishes the existence of an arbitration agreement. See Condee v. Longwood Management Corp. (2001) 88 Cal.App.4th 215, 218-219. Plaintiff fails to demonstrate unconscionability and the court severs the arbitration agreement's fee-splitting language. If defendant refuses to cover the fees, then the court will deny arbitration under Cinel v. Barna (2012) 206 Cal.App.4th 1383, 1389. In Little, 29 Cal.4th 1064, the court extended Armendariz and applied its requirements to employer-mandated arbitration of tort claims for wrongful discharge in violation of public policy (i.e., claims under Tameny v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (1980) 27 Cal.3d 167, 164). Justifying the extension, the court reasoned that Tameny claims, even though not statutory, are nevertheless "almost by definition unwaivable" (Little, at p. 1077) because they seek to enforce public policies that are carefully tethered to fundamental policies delineated in constitutional or statutory provisions. To extend the Armendariz requirements to Tameny claims was also consistent with the object of those requirements, which is "to ensure minimum standards of fairness in arbitration so that employees subject to mandatory arbitration agreements can vindicate their public rights in an arbitral forum." The same is true in this case. Plaintiff's claims include causes of action under the Labor Code to recover back wages (cause of action 13). The intentional tort (assault, battery) causes of action are subject to arbitration. The language of the parties' agreement along with the handbook is broad enough to compel arbitration of those claims and there is a public policy in favor of arbitration. Defendant correctly notes that a venue selection provision is enforceable under Battaglia v. Sup. Crt. (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 309, 317-319, where it does not conflict with the state venue statutes. Par. 7a, however, is not a venue selection clause. It states "[i]f the parties to the controversy cannot agree on three neutral arbitrators, the parties shall petition the San Diego County Superior Court for appointment of three neutral arbitrators." The agreement says nothing about where the actual arbitration shall occur. Therefore, the court grants arbitration, orders defendant to pay the extra-court fees under Armendariz, stays this case, and denies the motion to change venue. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)
